summary: Reporter finds that SVG output from SKSvgCanvas with SKManagedWStream is missing the closing </svg> tag because MemoryStream.ToArray() is called before the canvas is disposed — the SVG canvas writes the closing tag in its destructor.

stepsToReproduce: Create a MemoryStream, wrap in SKManagedWStream, create SKSvgCanvas with it
Call managedStream.ToArray() before the canvas using-var scope ends
Observe the resulting SVG is missing the closing </svg> tag
reproEvidence.environmentDetails: SkiaSharp 3.119.0, Blazor + iOS via Rider, all browser platforms affected

screenshots.description: SVG rendered without closing tag — broken output
SVG source showing missing closing tag
Correct rendering when closing tag is manually added

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The SVG canvas disposal behavior hasn't changed — the issue is in the reporter's disposal ordering which may have coincidentally worked differently in 2.x due to the older SKXmlWriter-based API.

regression.reason: In SkiaSharp 2.x, SKSvgCanvas used SKXmlWriter. The 3.x API changed to take a stream directly. The reporter's disposal ordering issue may not have manifested with the old API because the code pattern was different (explicit SKXmlWriter lifecycle). The underlying Skia behavior (write closing tag on destruction) has not changed.

analysis.summary: The SVG canvas writes the closing </svg> tag when the SKCanvas is disposed (destroyed). The reporter's code uses C# 'using var' declarations for all objects and calls MemoryStream.ToArray() before the end of the scope — before the canvas is disposed. The existing test SvgCanvasSavesFile demonstrates the correct pattern: dispose the canvas first, then read the stream.
analysis.rationale: Classified as type/bug because the reporter experiences broken output, but the root cause is disposal ordering in their code — the SVG canvas is designed to finalize the document on destruction. The existing test confirms this is by-design behavior. The reporter's comment that SKFileWStream works is consistent: file output is typically read after all disposals complete, while MemoryStream.ToArray() is called inline. Recommending close-as-by-design with a concrete workaround showing correct disposal ordering.

keySignals.text: using var managedStream = new MemoryStream(); using var stream = new SKManagedWStream(managedStream); using var canvas = SKSvgCanvas.Create(bounds, stream);
keySignals.interpretation: All three objects use 'using var' — disposal happens at end of scope, AFTER ToArray() is called.
Reading data before canvas disposal — the closing tag hasn't been written yet.

keySignals.text: Looking into it, it might be SKManagedWStream specific. SKFileWStream seems to output a correct SVG with close tags.
keySignals.interpretation: Consistent with disposal ordering: file output is read after disposal, MemoryStream is read inline before disposal.

keySignals.text: This seems to be a 'recent' issue as it certainly used to work
keySignals.interpretation: SkiaSharp 2.x used SKXmlWriter-based API with different lifecycle patterns — the disposal trap is specific to the 3.x stream-based API.

codeInvestigation.finding: SvgCanvasSavesFile test demonstrates correct pattern: canvas in a scoped 'using' block, stream read AFTER the block ends (after canvas disposal). This confirms the closing tag is written on disposal.
Two Create overloads: Stream overload creates an owned SKManagedWStream (disposed with canvas), SKWStream overload keeps a reference only. Both rely on canvas disposal to finalize SVG.
DisposeNative calls sk_canvas_destroy which triggers the C++ SkCanvas destructor — for SVG canvas, this writes the closing </svg> tag to the stream.
SKCanvas.Flush() only flushes the GPU context ((Context as GRContext)?.Flush()). It does NOT flush SVG content — no way to force the closing tag without disposing the canvas.
sk_svgcanvas_create_with_stream calls SkSVGCanvas::Make and releases ownership — the canvas owns the SVG writer and finalizes it on destruction.

workarounds: Dispose the SVG canvas before reading the stream: use a scoped 'using' block instead of 'using var', then call ToArray() after the block ends.
Use the Stream overload of SKSvgCanvas.Create (passing MemoryStream directly) and let SkiaSharp manage the SKManagedWStream lifecycle.

resolution.hypothesis: The SVG canvas writes the closing </svg> tag in its destructor. The reporter reads from the MemoryStream before the canvas is disposed because 'using var' defers disposal to end of scope.

proposals.title: Use scoped using block for canvas
proposals.description: Wrap the SVG canvas in a scoped using block so it's disposed before reading the stream. This matches the pattern used in the existing test.

proposals.title: Explicitly dispose canvas before reading
proposals.description: If scoped using blocks don't fit the code structure, explicitly call canvas.Dispose() before reading the stream data.

resolution.recommendedProposal: Use scoped using block for canvas
resolution.recommendedReason: Matches the pattern used in the existing test (SvgCanvasSavesFile). Cleaner than explicit Dispose() and avoids double-dispose concerns.

actionability.reason: The closing SVG tag is written on canvas disposal — this is Skia's documented behavior. The reporter's code reads the stream before disposing the canvas. The existing test confirms the correct pattern.

actions.description: Keep type/bug, add area and platform labels
Explain disposal ordering and provide workaround
Close as by-design behavior with workaround provided
